hardware.name: Hardware
hardware.description: Consumer and creative-tech hardware that shifted what artists could build

web-standards.name: Web Standards
web-standards.description: Browser APIs and graphics standards that enabled new web-native creative work

ai-ml.name: AI / ML
ai-ml.description: Machine learning models, releases, and tools that opened new creative territory

artworks.name: Landmark Artworks
artworks.description: Notable works that defined or expanded what creative coding could be

institutions.name: Institutions & Communities
institutions.description: Schools, festivals, and communities that shaped the field

publications.name: Publications
publications.description: Blogs, magazines, and platforms that documented and circulated the field's work

platforms.name: Platforms
platforms.description: Networks and services that changed how creative-tech work was shared and found

events.title: iPhone launches
events.description: First mass-market multitouch device. Reshaped expectations for interactive, gestural interfaces.

events.title: iPad launches
events.description: Brought multitouch to a canvas-sized device; widely adopted for sketching, performance, and installation control surfaces.

events.title: Microsoft Kinect (Xbox 360)
events.description: Consumer depth camera. Hacks within hours; OpenKinect drivers within weeks; an entire era of installation work followed.

events.title: Official Kinect SDK for Windows
events.description: Microsoft releases an official SDK after the OpenKinect community led the way.

events.title: Leap Motion ships
events.description: Sub-millimeter hand tracking in a desktop peripheral. Used in countless gestural installations and prototypes.

events.title: Oculus Rift DK1 ships
events.description: First widely available developer kit for modern consumer VR. Kickstarted the second VR wave.

events.title: Oculus Rift CV1 / HTC Vive (consumer VR)
events.description: Consumer VR ships. Vive adds room-scale tracking. VR becomes a viable medium for artists.

events.title: Meta Quest 2
events.description: Standalone, untethered VR at a consumer price. Made VR work radically more shippable.

events.title: Apple Vision Pro
events.description: Apple's first spatial computing device. Reframes XR around passthrough and eye/hand input.

events.title: WebGL 1.0 ratified
events.description: Khronos releases WebGL 1.0 spec. GPU-accelerated graphics in the browser becomes a baseline assumption.

events.title: WebRTC enters browsers
events.description: Peer-to-peer audio/video/data in the browser. Enables networked performance and telepresence work.

events.title: WebAssembly 1.0
events.description: Near-native code execution in browsers. Ports of C/C++ creative tooling become feasible.

events.title: WebGL 2.0 ships
events.description: OpenGL ES 3.0-equivalent in the browser. Transform feedback, instanced rendering, more.

events.title: WebXR Device API
events.description: Standard browser API for VR/AR content. Makes browser-based immersive work portable across headsets.

events.title: WebGPU ships in Chrome
events.description: Modern GPU API (compute + graphics) in the browser. Successor to WebGL.

events.title: TensorFlow open-sourced
events.description: Google releases TensorFlow. ML moves from research papers into reach of practitioners.

events.title: PyTorch released
events.description: Facebook AI Research releases PyTorch. Becomes the de facto ML research framework.

events.title: StyleGAN released
events.description: NVIDIA's StyleGAN. Convincing synthetic faces; a flood of generative art followed.

events.title: DALL·E announced
events.description: OpenAI shows text-to-image generation at quality that surprised even ML researchers.

events.title: Midjourney public beta
events.description: Discord-based text-to-image generator opens publicly. Mainstream creative use of generative AI begins.

events.title: Stable Diffusion released
events.description: Open-weights text-to-image model. Generative AI becomes runnable on a laptop GPU.

events.title: ChatGPT released
events.description: Conversational LLM hits 100M users in two months. Reshapes the entire creative-tech conversation.

events.title: GPT-4 released
events.description: Multimodal model with substantially stronger reasoning. Becomes a building block for creative tools.

events.title: OpenAI Sora announced
events.description: Text-to-video at minute-long durations with surprising temporal coherence.

events.title: Camille Utterback & Romy Achituv — Text Rain
events.description: Falling letters interact with the silhouette of viewers via real-time video. A canonical interactive installation.

events.title: Daniel Rozin — Wooden Mirror
events.description: Hundreds of motorized wooden tiles reflect viewers as a low-resolution image. Defined an aesthetic of physical pixels.

events.title: Aaron Koblin — Flight Patterns
events.description: FAA flight data rendered as luminous lines. An early viral example of creative coding meeting data.

events.title: Random International — Rain Room
events.description: Walk through rain that stops where your body is. Installation work goes mainstream museum.

events.title: Janet Echelman & Aaron Koblin — Unnumbered Sparks
events.description: Massive suspended sculpture painted in real time by audience phones via Chrome and WebGL.

events.title: Memo Akten — Learning to See
events.description: Real-time pix2pix translates everyday objects into landscapes/clouds/fire. An early landmark of artistic ML.

events.title: teamLab Borderless opens (Tokyo)
events.description: Permanent immersive digital-art museum. Set a global reference point for room-scale generative installations.

events.title: Refik Anadol — Machine Hallucinations
events.description: Large-scale GAN-driven projections on architecture. Brought generative ML art to mainstream institutions.

events.title: ITP at NYU founded
events.description: NYU's Interactive Telecommunications Program. Long-running incubator for creative technologists.

events.title: MIT Media Lab opens
events.description: Founded by Nicholas Negroponte and Jerome Wiesner. Many creative coding tools and ideas trace through here.

events.title: Gray Area founded (San Francisco)
events.description: Nonprofit fusing art and technology; long-running creative code education and a key US community hub.

events.title: Eyeo Festival begins
events.description: Annual Minneapolis festival convening artists, designers, and technologists. Defined a generation's creative-code conversation.

events.title: Resonate Festival begins (Belgrade)
events.description: European counterpart to Eyeo; key gathering for digital culture and creative practice.

events.title: School for Poetic Computation
events.description: Artist-run school in NYC for code as a creative and critical practice.

events.title: E.A.T. (Experiments in Art and Technology) founded
events.description: Nonprofit founded by engineers and artists to broker collaborations between them, growing out of the 1966 '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' performances. Never formally dissolved; Kluver led it until his death in 2004.

events.title: Experimental Television Center founded
events.description: New York State center that gave video artists open access to experimental image-processing tools through a long-running residency program. The residency and grants program was paused in 2011 to focus on preservation; the archive later moved to Atlanta (2021).

events.title: Eyebeam founded
events.description: New York nonprofit art and technology center supporting artists working experimentally with technology through residencies, fellowships, and open-source production. Its Chelsea space opened in 2001.

events.title: F.A.T. Lab (Free Art and Technology)
events.description: Online collective devoted to enriching the public domain through open-source pop culture hacks, blending art, technology activism, and internet humor. Dissolved 1 August 2015.

events.title: CreativeApplications.Net launches
events.description: Publication reporting on creative coding, digital art, and critical making that became a defining record of the field's projects and tools.

events.title: Prosthetic Knowledge
events.description: Anonymously run Tumblr that curated and popularized art, technology, and computing projects. Last post 5 September 2018; the archive remains online. Start date is derived rather than stated: a 2019 anniversary tweet from the operator places it at December 2009.

events.title: The Creators Project launches
events.description: VICE and Intel partnership that funded, documented, and exhibited artists working at the intersection of creativity and technology. Rebranded 'Creators' c. 2016 and wound down as VICE consolidated its channels; the end date is approximate.

events.title: Twitter launches publicly
events.description: Microblogging service whose short-message, real-time public feed became core infrastructure for how the creative coding and digital art communities shared work and found each other. Rebranded to X in 2023.

events.title: First SIGGRAPH conference
events.description: First SIGGRAPH conference, held in Boulder, Colorado, drawing roughly 600 attendees. The ACM special interest group behind it formed in 1969; the conference became the venue where foundational rendering, animation, and interaction techniques were first published.

events.title: First Ars Electronica festival
events.description: First festival in Linz, Austria, establishing the template for the art/technology/society festival and giving electronic art a serious public venue decades before museums took it up. The Prix Ars Electronica followed in 1987; the Ars Electronica Center opened in 1996.

events.title: transmediale begins (as VideoFilmFest)
events.description: Began as VideoFilmFest, a video-art side programme of the Berlinale, and grew into Europe's key festival for critical discourse on digital culture. Renamed VideoFest in 1989, transmedia in 1997, and transmediale in 1998.

events.title: Rhizome founded
events.description: Founded as an email list for net.art discussion and incorporated as a nonprofit in 1998; affiliated with the New Museum since 2003. Its ArtBase archive preserves born-digital work that would otherwise have rotted away.

events.title: Vimeo launches
events.description: Public launch of the video platform built as a side project inside CollegeHumor's parent company. Its early move to HD (2007) and Staff Picks curation (2008) made it the default home for motion design, experimental film, and creative-coding documentation. Founded 14 November 2004.

events.title: YouTube opens in public beta
events.description: Site opened to the public; Jawed Karim's 'Me at the zoo' was uploaded the same day. Made publishing video to a global audience free and frictionless, collapsing the distribution barrier for moving-image work. Domain registered 14 February 2005; exited beta 15 December 2005.

events.title: GitHub launches publicly
events.description: Made version control a social, public act, letting the open-source creative-coding ecosystem - openFrameworks, Processing libraries, three.js, shader collections - become forkable and cumulative rather than scattered across personal sites. Company founded 8 February 2008.

events.title: Visible Language Workshop founded
events.description: Muriel Cooper's Bauhaus-style research atelier at MIT, founded with designer Ron MacNeil after she left the MIT Press, which became one of the MIT Media Lab's founding research groups in 1985 and pioneered dynamic three-dimensional information display. It is the institutional origin of the MIT design-computation lineage that runs VLW to the Aesthetics + Computation Group to Design By Numbers to Processing. Founding is variously dated 1973 (the 'Messages and Means' course), 1974, or 1975 (the MIT 150 Exhibition label); the end date is Cooper's death in May 1994, as there was no formal dissolution.

events.title: قلب (Qalb) — Ramsey Nasser
events.description: A functional programming language written entirely in Arabic script, made as a critical artwork rather than a production tool. Qalb (Arabic for 'heart'/'core') exposes how deeply programming assumes English, the Latin alphabet, and left-to-right ASCII — Nasser found that even rendering his own right-to-left code was broken by that assumption. Debuted at Eyebeam's Annual Artist Showcase and accompanied by a calligraphy series treating classic computer-science algorithms as visual art.

events.title: Radical Networks begins
events.description: A community event and conference for critical, artistic, and grassroots work in telecommunications and networking, founded by Sarah Grant. The first edition ran October 24–25, 2015 at MAGNET (NYU Poly) in Brooklyn; later editions were also held in Berlin. Foregrounds DIY, feminist, and activist approaches to the internet and network infrastructure outside corporate and academic mainstreams.

events.title: Vera Molnár — Interruptions
events.description: Plotter-drawn generative series: a regular grid of short line segments randomly rotated, with programmed 'interruptions' leaving blank gaps. Among the earliest and most influential works of computer-plotter generative art. Molnár began her computer/plotter work in 1968; collected drawings are dated 1969.

events.title: Analívia Cordeiro — M3x3
events.description: A computer-choreographed dance for television: a FORTRAN program on a UNICAMP mainframe generated positions and timings for nine dancers on a 3×3 m grid, synced to camera moves. Widely credited as the first Brazilian video art and a landmark of early algorithmic choreography.

events.title: Lillian Schwartz — Pixillation
events.description: A four-minute computer-assisted animation made during Schwartz's Bell Labs residency, combining machine-generated pixel patterns (via Ken Knowlton's EXPLOR language) with hand-colored abstraction and a Moog score. An early landmark of computer film.

events.title: Katherine Nash & Richard Williams — ART1
events.description: One of the earliest art-making software tools designed for artists without programming skills: ~350 FORTRAN statements invoked by a single command. Developed at the University of New Mexico ~1968 and published in Leonardo (Oct 1970), the publication that popularized accessible computer art.

events.title: Joan Truckenbrod — Entropic Tangle
events.description: An early algorithmic plotter drawing coded in FORTRAN, using variables and mathematical modulation to simulate invisible natural forces. Part of Truckenbrod's Coded Algorithmic Drawings, among the first artist uses of programming for algorithmic drawing. Date is the coding date; first-exhibition date is undocumented.

events.title: Rebecca Allen — The Catherine Wheel
events.description: 3D computer-animated character (St. Catherine) created for Twyla Tharp's dance film with music by David Byrne — cited as one of the first and most intricate examples of 3D computer-generated human motion, and the first aired on television.

events.title: Nam June Paik — TV Garden
events.description: Immersive installation of dozens of televisions nestled face-up among live tropical plants, playing Paik's 'Global Groove.' A foundational work of video-art environment by the Korean-American pioneer of the medium. First realized 1974 at Galeria Bonino, New York.

events.title: Rafael Lozano-Hemmer — Body Movies
events.description: Large-scale interactive urban projection in which xenon-lit passersby cast giant shadows that reveal thousands of street portraits on a building façade. A landmark of participatory 'relational architecture' by the Mexican-Canadian artist; premiered in Rotterdam.

events.title: Zach Blas — Facial Weaponization Suite
events.description: A series of amorphous masks generated from the aggregate facial data of participants, designed to defeat biometric facial recognition. A defining work of queer, anti-surveillance media art; first mask/video 2012, public scanning deployments 2013.

events.title: Sougwen Chung — Drawing Operations
events.description: Live drawing performance in which the artist collaborates with a robotic arm ('D.O.U.G.') that mimics her gestures in real time via computer vision. A foundational work of human–robot artistic co-creation; first performed 2015 at NEW INC, New York.

events.title: Mimi Onuoha — The Library of Missing Datasets
events.description: A filing cabinet whose labeled folders are all empty, cataloguing 'missing datasets' — information deliberately or negligently uncollected. A key critical-data artwork on absence and bias; exists in several versions from 2016.

events.title: Lauren Lee McCarthy — LAUREN
events.description: Participatory performance in which the artist becomes a live, human version of a smart-home AI assistant, watching over volunteers' homes 24/7 via installed devices — a critique of surveillance, care, and automation. By the creator of p5.js.

events.title: Morehshin Allahyari — Material Speculation: ISIS
events.description: Series of 3D-printed reconstructions of ancient artifacts destroyed by ISIS, each embedding a memory card of research files — reclamation as resistance and a critique of 'digital colonialism' by the Iranian-American artist. Full-series debut Toronto, Feb 2016 (research from 2015).

events.title: American Artist — Black Gooey Universe
events.description: Exhibition and body of work interrogating the anti-Blackness embedded in the white default of the graphical user interface ('gooey'), including tarred workstations and shattered-screen grids. Developed during an Eyebeam residency; first shown at HOUSING, Brooklyn.

events.title: Sondra Perry — Typhoon coming on
events.description: Immersive installation built around a glitch-animated reworking of Turner's 'Slave Ship' rendered in Blender's default error-warning purple, linking the transatlantic slave trade to digital error and Black identity. Premiered at the Serpentine, London.

events.title: VNS Matrix — A Cyberfeminist Manifesto for the 21st Century
events.description: The founding text of cyberfeminism, in which the term first appeared; a short, deliberately provocative manifesto claiming women's place in digital and networked space. Distributed across billboard, print, radio and early net from Adelaide/Sydney, Australia.

events.title: Rosa Menkman — Glitch Studies Manifesto
events.description: A short manifesto reframing digital glitches, compression artifacts and noise as legitimate aesthetic and critical material rather than error. Foundational to glitch studies as an artistic and academic field. Self-published PDF 2010; first print 2011 (Video Vortex Reader II).

events.title: Legacy Russell — Glitch Feminism Manifesto
events.description: The originating essay of 'glitch feminism,' arguing the digital glitch is a site of refusal and liberation for raced, gendered and queer bodies. First published on the Cyborgology blog; later expanded into the book Glitch Feminism (Verso, 2020).

events.title: Mindy Seu — Cyberfeminism Index
events.description: A crowdsourced index of 700+ entries documenting radical techno-critical and cyberfeminist activism across net art, hackerspaces, activism and theory. Launched as a Rhizome-commissioned website (2020); print edition Inventory Press (2023).

events.title: STEIM founded (Amsterdam)
events.description: Amsterdam research and development center for electronic musical instruments and live-electronic performance (home of the Cracklebox and Michel Waisvisz's 'The Hands'), founded 1969 by seven Dutch composers. Ceased operating as an organization at the end of 2020 after national funding cuts.

events.title: V2_ Lab for the Unstable Media founded
events.description: Interdisciplinary center for art and media technology, founded 1981 in 's-Hertogenbosch and based in Rotterdam since 1994; originator of the 'Manifesto for the Unstable Media' (1987) and organizer of the Dutch Electronic Art Festival (DEAF).

events.title: Festival Videobrasil begins (São Paulo)
events.description: Pioneering video- and electronic-art festival founded 1983 in São Paulo by Solange Farkas, created to platform video and electronic art across Brazil and the global South; later the Contemporary Art Biennial Sesc_Videobrasil.

events.title: First International Symposium on Electronic Art (FISEA)
events.description: The founding edition of the roaming International Symposium on Electronic Art, held in Utrecht, Netherlands; initiated by Theo Hesper and Wim van der Plas. ISEA became the leading international gathering for electronic and digital art. (The standing Inter-Society was formed 1990.)

events.title: ZKM | Center for Art and Media founded (Karlsruhe)
events.description: Media-art center in Karlsruhe, Germany, combining museums, research institutes and production studios; founding director Heinrich Klotz. Legally founded 1989; opened its permanent home in a former munitions factory in 1997.

events.title: WRO Media Art Biennale begins (Wrocław)
events.description: The first review of video and new-media art in Poland and Central/Eastern Europe, launched December 1989 in Wrocław as the 'Sound Basis Visual Art Festival,' weeks after the fall of the Berlin Wall; biennial since 1993.

events.title: NTT InterCommunication Center (ICC) opens (Tokyo)
events.description: Media-art museum and research center in Nishi-Shinjuku, Tokyo, established by telecom NTT to mark the centenary of telephone service in Japan; opened 1997 with a focus on VR, interactive and network art.

events.title: Yamaguchi Center for Arts and Media (YCAM) opens
events.description: Municipal arts and media center in Yamaguchi, Japan (building by Arata Isozaki), opened 2003; known for commissioning and producing new interactive media artworks, performance, and R&D (YCAM InterLab) rather than holding a collection.

events.title: FILE — Electronic Language International Festival begins
events.description: New-media/electronic-art festival founded 2000 in São Paulo by Ricardo Barreto and Paula Perissinotto; grew into the largest electronic-art event in Latin America.

events.title: MUTEK begins (Montreal)
events.description: Festival of electronic music and digital creativity founded 2000 in Montreal by Alain Mongeau; a long-running international platform for audiovisual performance with editions across several countries.

events.title: Dorkbot founded (New York)
events.description: Grassroots, globally-franchised series of informal meetings for 'people doing strange things with electricity' — artists, engineers and hackers presenting electronic and computational art. Begun at Columbia University by Douglas Repetto; spread to 100+ cities.

events.title: Old Boys Network founded (Berlin)
events.description: The first international cyberfeminist alliance, based in Berlin; organized the First (1997), Next (1999) and Very (2001) Cyberfeminist Internationals and produced the '100 Anti-Theses' of cyberfeminism.

events.title: subRosa formed
events.description: A U.S.-based 'reproducible cyberfeminist cell' combining art, activism and politics to critique the effects of information and biotechnologies on women's bodies, labor and reproduction, largely through participatory performance.

events.title: GLI.TC/H festival begins (Chicago)
events.description: International glitch-art festival and conference founded in Chicago in 2010 (workshops, screenings, performances, gallery shows); the 2011 edition traveled Chicago–Amsterdam–Birmingham. Three editions ran, 2010–2012.

events.title: TOPLAP founded (Hamburg)
events.description: The informal international organization founded to link and promote live-coding communities, as an offshoot of the Changing Grammars symposium in Hamburg. Its 'Read Me' manifesto (published 2004 in the Read_Me: Software Art and Cultures anthology) set the movement's central tenet — that performers' code be projected and shown to the audience ('show us your screens').

events.title: First Algorave (London)
events.description: The first event named 'algorave,' where music is generated from algorithms written and modified live (often with projected code), held in London as a warm-up for the SuperCollider Symposium 2012. It seeded a global network of algorave events.

events.title: Processing Foundation established
events.description: Nonprofit founded in 2012 by Casey Reas, Ben Fry and Daniel Shiffman to promote software literacy in the visual arts and visual literacy in technology, and to widen access for communities historically without it. Fiscal and organizational home of Processing, p5.js and ml5.js.

events.title: Deep Lab founded
events.description: A congress and collective of cyberfeminist researchers, artists, engineers and journalists examining privacy, surveillance, anonymity and data aggregation, founded by Addie Wagenknecht at Carnegie Mellon's Frank-Ratchye STUDIO for Creative Inquiry; produced a collaboratively written book and lecture series.

events.title: bitforms gallery opens (New York)
events.description: One of the first commercial galleries devoted to new media and digital art, founded by Steven Sacks in New York (originally Chelsea, later the Lower East Side). Represents artists critically engaged with new technology, from Manfred Mohr and Rafael Lozano-Hemmer to Casey Reas and Refik Anadol.

events.title: TRANSFER gallery opens (Brooklyn)
events.description: Experimental gallery for computer-based and internet art founded by Kelani Nichole in Brooklyn, showing digital work that extends beyond the screen into physical space and foregrounding women artists refiguring technology.
